title.en: await and asyncio.gather: Running Things Concurrently

description.en: You wrote async code, sprinkled await everywhere, and it's no faster than the blocking version. The reason: awaiting coroutines one by one is still sequential. Learn what await really does, why awaits in a loop are slow for I/O, and how asyncio.gather schedules many coroutines at once and returns their results in order.

story_variants.en:
## The Slow Async Program

You converted your code to `async`. You `await` every network call. And yet fetching ten URLs takes ten times as long as fetching one. You did everything the tutorial said, so why is it sequential?

Because `await` does not mean "run this in the background." It means **"pause here until this finishes, and let the event loop do something else while I wait."** If there is nothing else *scheduled*, the loop has nothing else to do — so it just waits for your one call, then moves to the next line. Ten awaits in a row is ten waits in a row.

## What `await` Actually Does

A coroutine is a paused-able function. When you write `await something`, three things happen:

1. The current coroutine **suspends** at that point.
2. Control returns to the **event loop**, which is free to run any *other* ready coroutine.
3. When the awaited thing completes, the loop **resumes** your coroutine right where it paused, with the result.

The critical detail: `await` only yields control. It does **not** start any other work for you. Calling `square(1)` creates a coroutine object — but nothing runs until something *awaits* it or *schedules* it.

## Sequential awaits vs gather

Look at the difference:

```python
# Sequential: each await finishes before the next starts
r1 = await fetch(a)   # wait...
r2 = await fetch(b)   # ...then wait again
r3 = await fetch(c)   # ...then wait again
# total time ~= a + b + c
```

```python
# Concurrent: schedule all three, then wait once for all
r1, r2, r3 = await asyncio.gather(fetch(a), fetch(b), fetch(c))
# total time ~= max(a, b, c)
```

`asyncio.gather(*coros)` takes several awaitables, **schedules them all on the loop immediately**, and gives you a single awaitable that completes when *all* of them are done. While one is waiting on I/O, the others make progress. That is real concurrency.

| | Sequential awaits | `asyncio.gather(*coros)` |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduling | One at a time, on demand | All at once, eagerly |
| Total time (I/O) | Sum of every wait | About the longest single wait |
| Result order | Source order (you assign each) | **Input order**, not finish order |
| Failure | Stops at the failing await | Propagates first exception (or `return_exceptions=True`) |
| Best for | Steps that depend on each other | Independent I/O tasks |

## Order Is Preserved

The result you get back from `gather` is a list lined up with the **order you passed the coroutines in** — never the order they happened to finish. So `await asyncio.gather(square(1), square(2), square(3))` is always `[1, 4, 9]`, even if `square(3)` somehow finished first. You can rely on positional results without tracking which task completed when.

## Scheduling Eagerly with create_task

`asyncio.create_task(coro)` is the lower-level building block: it wraps a coroutine in a **Task** and schedules it on the loop *right now*, returning immediately. `gather` uses this under the hood. Use `create_task` when you want a coroutine running in the background while you do other things, then `await` the task later for its result.

```python
t = asyncio.create_task(fetch(a))   # starts now
... do other work ...
result = await t                    # collect when needed
```

## The One Big Caveat: I/O Only

Concurrency here comes entirely from coroutines **yielding while they wait**. `gather` makes things faster only when the work is **I/O-bound** — network, disk, sleeping — because that is when a coroutine hands control back to the loop.

For **CPU-bound** work (crunching numbers in pure Python), there is no waiting and nothing to yield. Gathering CPU-bound coroutines does **not** speed them up — they still run one after another on a single thread, and you've added overhead for nothing. For CPU work you need threads or processes, not `gather`. Async buys you concurrency for *waiting*, not for *computing*.

> **Key idea:** `await` pauses the current coroutine and yields control to the event loop, but it does not start other work — so awaiting coroutines one by one is sequential. `asyncio.gather(*coros)` schedules them all at once and waits for all, returning results **in input order** regardless of who finished first. This is a win only for I/O-bound work, where coroutines yield while they wait; it does nothing for CPU-bound work.

nodes.label: await + gather
children: await: pause coroutine, yield to the event loop
await does NOT start other work for you
Sequential awaits = one wait after another
gather(*coros): schedule all, wait for all
gather returns results in INPUT order
Helps I/O-bound work only, not CPU-bound

left.label: Sequential awaits: total time = sum of every wait (a + b + c)

right.label: asyncio.gather(*coros): all scheduled at once, total time = the longest single wait

description.en: For independent I/O tasks, gather overlaps the waiting. Results still come back in the order you passed the coroutines in.

animation_sequence.code:
import asyncio

async def square(n):
    return n * n

async def main():
    return await asyncio.gather(square(1), square(2), square(3))

print(asyncio.run(main()))

executionSteps.description: gather schedules every coroutine on the loop at once.
All complete; results come back in INPUT order, not finish order.

output: await = pause + yield to the loop. It does NOT start other work.
Awaiting one-by-one is sequential; gather(*coros) runs them concurrently.
gather returns results in INPUT order — and only helps I/O-bound work.

instruction.en: Run several coroutines concurrently and collect their results in order. Given async def square(n): return n * n, finish main() so it gathers square(1), square(2), and square(3) with a single asyncio.gather call and returns their results. gather preserves INPUT order, so the result must be [1, 4, 9] — not whatever order they finished in. Expected output: [1, 4, 9]

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import asyncio

async def square(n):
    return n * n

async def main():
    # TODO: schedule all three coroutines concurrently with ONE asyncio.gather
    # call and return the list of results (in input order).
    results = await asyncio.gather()  # fix this line
    return results

print(asyncio.run(main()))

en: Pass every coroutine to a single gather call: asyncio.gather(square(1), square(2), square(3)).
gather returns an awaitable, so you must await it: results = await asyncio.gather(...).
The results list lines up with the ORDER you passed the coroutines in, so square(1) -> 1, square(2) -> 4, square(3) -> 9.
asyncio.run(main()) drives the event loop and prints the returned list [1, 4, 9].

practice_challenges.test_code:
import asyncio
result = asyncio.run(main())
assert result == [1, 4, 9], result
# gather preserves INPUT order regardless of completion order:
async def _g(*coros):
    return await asyncio.gather(*coros)
assert asyncio.run(_g(square(3), square(1), square(2))) == [9, 1, 4]
assert asyncio.run(_g(square(2), square(1))) == [4, 1]

en: It runs the awaited coroutine in a background thread
It pauses the current coroutine and yields control to the event loop until the awaited thing completes
It starts every other coroutine in the program
It blocks the whole interpreter until the result is ready

q.en: Why is awaiting three I/O coroutines one after another slow?

en: await secretly adds extra delays between calls
Each await finishes before the next starts, so the total time is the sum of all three waits
Coroutines can only run one per second
The event loop refuses to run more than one coroutine ever
Results in finish order, which is unpredictable
[9, 1, 4] — results in the INPUT order you passed the coroutines
[1, 4, 9] — always sorted ascending
A single coroutine you still have to await again

q.en: For which kind of work does asyncio.gather actually make things faster?

en: CPU-bound work, because gather runs coroutines on multiple cores
I/O-bound work, because coroutines yield to the loop while they wait so the waits overlap
Any work at all, since gather always parallelizes
Only work wrapped in asyncio.create_task, never plain coroutines
